---
title: "LECTURE_TITLE"
subtitle: "MODULE_NAME - Lecture N"
author: "Erik An"
date: last-modified
format:
  live-html:
    theme:
      light: flatly
      dark: darkly
    css: ../../styles.css
engine: knitr
toc: true
---

{{< include ../../_extensions/r-wasm/live/_knitr.qmd >}}

<!-- ============================================================
     MFDS LECTURE TEMPLATE
     ============================================================

     STRUCTURE
     ---------
     Every lecture follows the same seven-section arc.
     Delete the comments once you've filled in your content.

       §1  Motivation     — Why should I care?
       §2  Theory         — Definitions, theorems, derivations.
       §3  Implementation — Interactive code that builds the idea.
       §4  Experiment     — Tweak parameters, observe behaviour.
       §5  Visualisation  — A plot that consolidates intuition.
       §6  Edge cases     — Where does the method break?
       §7  Exercise       — One exercise the reader solves in-browser.
       §8  Summary        — Callout box + bullet recap.

     CONVENTIONS
     -----------
     • One concept per lecture.  Aim for 15–25 min reading time.
     • Keep prose between code blocks short (2–4 sentences).
     • Every pyodide block must be self-contained OR depend only
       on blocks that appear earlier in the same lecture.
     • Use `---` horizontal rules to give visual breathing room.
     • Callout types:  .callout-note   (blue)   — formulas, recalls
                       .callout-tip    (green)  — intuition, tricks
                       .callout-warning(yellow) — pitfalls
                       .callout-important(red)  — critical caveats
     ============================================================ -->


## 1. Motivation

<!-- 2–3 sentences: a concrete problem that creates the *need* for
     the concept.  End with a specific example the rest of the
     lecture will solve. -->


---

## 2. Theory

<!-- Definitions → key formula → short derivation.
     Wrap the main result in a callout: -->

::: {.callout-note}
## Main Result
$$
  \text{FORMULA HERE}
$$
:::


---

## 3. Implementation

<!-- First block: imports + helper definitions.
     Second block: the core algorithm.
     Keep blocks short (≤ 25 lines). -->

```{pyodide}
import numpy as np
```

```{pyodide}
# Core algorithm here
```


---

## 4. Experiment

<!-- One interactive block where the reader changes a parameter
     (starting point, step size, tolerance …) and sees the effect
     printed as a table or short log. -->

```{pyodide}
# === EXPERIMENT: Change ... and re-run! ===
```


---

## 5. Visualisation

```{pyodide}
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt

# One clear, well-labelled plot.
```


---

## 6. Edge Cases

<!-- A short block that demonstrates *failure* or surprising
     behaviour.  Follow with 1–2 sentences of explanation. -->

```{pyodide}
# What happens when …?
```


---

## 7. Exercise

<!-- State the task in bold, give a hint, then provide a scaffold
     with TODO markers for the reader to fill in. -->

**Your task:** …

*Hint:* …

```{pyodide}
# TODO: ...
```


---

## 8. Summary

::: {.callout-note}
## Key Formula
$$
  \text{FORMULA HERE}
$$
:::

**Takeaways**

- …
- …
- …
